retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Introduction -- Liver Anatomy and Histology
retrieved_docs.content: The Couinaud classification subdivides the liver into 8 functionally independent segments based on vascularization, bile duct distribution, and lymphatic drainage. These wedge-shaped segments have apices directed toward the hepatic hilum and receive a single branch each of the bile duct, portal vein, and hepatic artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Chemoembolization -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The liver embryologically develops into lobes and segments. Several classification schemes describe these segments, but the most widely used is the Couinaud classification. In this classification, the segments are defined by the combination of the following components: Systemic (hepatic) draining vein The third order (third branch) portal vein supplying these segments with blood from the intestines and spleen

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Radiofrequency Ablation of Liver Tumors -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are multiple classification schemes for describing the various portions of the liver; the Couinaud system is the most widely used. According to Couinaud, the liver is divided into eight independent functional sections. Each section has its own portal pedicle comprised of a branch of the right or left hepatic artery, a branch of the portal vein, a hepatic venous branch, and a bile duct. The sections are numbered in a clockwise manner. The caudate lobe is segment I. Segment II is the anterior segment of the left lobe, and segment III is the posterior segment of the left lobe. The medial segment of the left lobe is segment IV. Together, segments II, III, and IV represent the left lobe of the liver. Conversely, the right lobe is comprised of segments V and VIII anteriorly and segments VI and VII posteriorly. Classification of these various segments of liver anatomy is important to both radiologists and surgeons alike, especially for preprocedural localization of focal hepatic lesions. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The most widely used anatomic classification of hepatic segments is the Couinaud classification, which describes 8 functionally independent liver segments based on vascularization, bile duct distribution, and lymphatic drainage. [6] Each segment is wedge-shaped, with the apex directed towards the hepatic hilum with some variability. [7] A single branch of the hepatic artery, portal vein, and bile duct enters through the apex into the hepatic segments. The hepatic vein runs between 2 adjacent segments and drains into the inferior vena cava (IVC). The middle hepatic vein runs from the inferior vena cava to the gallbladder fossa and divides the liver into the right and left lobes. [8] The right hepatic vein divides the right lobe into the anterior and posterior segments, and the falciform ligament divides the left lobe into medial and lateral segments. The portal vein divides segments horizontally into superior and inferior segments. Segment I is the unique caudate lobe, which can receive dual blood supply from the right and left portal vein and drains directly into IVC. Segments II, III, and IV are left hepatic lobe sections. Segments V, VI, VII, and VIII constitute the right lobe hepatic sections. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Radiofrequency Ablation of Liver Tumors -- Technique or Treatment -- Contrast-enhanced CT
retrieved_docs.content: Ingrowth: Enhancing tissue inside the border of the treated lesion, denoting inward growth of the tumor. Outgrowth: Enhancing tissue expanding outward from (but contiguous with) the border of the treated lesion, denoting outward growth of the tumor. Spread: New enhancing tissue separate from the treated lesion but contained within the same Couinaud liver segment Progression: New enhancing tissue seen in different Couinaud liver segments from the treated lesion

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Transplantation -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, the largest organ in the human body, lies beneath the eighth through twelfth ribs on the right side. [5] Anatomically, it consists of 4 lobes: the right and left lobes (divided by the falciform ligament), the quadrate lobe, and the caudate lobe. However, these are not true functional lobes; functionally, the liver is divided into right and left lobes by the Cantlie line, which runs through the gallbladder bed and the notch of the inferior vena cava. [5] [6] Each lobe is subdivided into 2 segments, and these are further divided into subsegments based on hepatic arterial and portal venous blood supply, biliary drainage, and hepatic venous outflow. The segments are numbered 1 to 8 in the Couinaud classification, with the caudate lobe designated as segment 1 and the rest numbered clockwise. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Falciform Ligament -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The falciform ligament forms a double-layered extension of the parietal peritoneum that arises from the anterior abdominal wall and divides the liver into asymmetric left and right lobes (see Image . Liver Segmental Anatomy and the Couinaud System). [1] [2] This ligament consists of 2 margins, 2 surfaces, an apex, and a base.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Liver ultrasonography (US), computed tomography (CT), and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are the primary imaging modalities to diagnose liver lesions. Postcontrast imaging can help distinguish lesions depending on their degree of vascularity and composition. Postcontrast hepatic imaging falls into 3 distinct phases: the arterial phase, the portal venous phase, and the delayed venous phase. [4] Ultrasound can be a method of choice as a screening modality, and contrast-enhanced multidetector CT (MDCT) is a modality of choice in most hepatic imaging. [5] MRI plays a role in better-characterizing lesions with equivocal features on US and CT.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Liver -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The functional unit of the liver is the lobule. Liver lobules are collections of hepatocytes in a hexagonal shape, with the center being a central vein. Within the lobules, the hepatocytes are arranged in cords, and in between the cords is a vascular space with a thin fenestrated endothelium and a discontinuous membrane called a sinusoid. These sinusoids contain Kupffer cells, the resident macrophage of the liver, and stellate cells, which are hepatic lipocytes. At the vertices of the hexagon is a triad of a bile duct branch, a portal vein branch, and a hepatic artery branch, referred to as a portal triad. Blood flows from the portal vein branch and hepatic artery branch across the lobule and finally into the central vein, a branch of the hepatic vein. An alternative organization of hepatocytes places the line between 2 triads at the center of a rhomboid, with the ends being central veins. This arrangement is called the portal acinus and helps describe the functional zones of the liver. Zone 1 hepatocytes immediately surround the portal tracts and are primarily involved in oxidative energy metabolism. Zone 3 hepatocytes immediately surround the central veins and are the primary location for the biotransformation of drugs. Zone 2 hepatocytes lie between zones 1 and 3 and have mixed functionality.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Introduction -- Liver Anatomy and Histology
retrieved_docs.content: The hepatic lobule serves as the primary functional unit, comprising hexagonal arrays of hepatocytes surrounding central veins. Hepatocytes organize into cords within lobules, enveloping sinusoids housing Kupffer and stellate cells. The lobule's organization into portal triads ensures efficient blood flow across zones delineated by their proximity to portal tracts or central veins. This alternative organization, known as the portal acinus, delineates functional zones. Zone 1 surrounds portal tracts involved in oxidative metabolism. Zone 3 envelopes central veins primarily engaged in drug biotransformation. Zone 2 exhibits mixed functionality.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The hepatic blood supply is from the portal vein (about two-thirds), and the rest is from the hepatic artery; thus, maximum enhancement of the liver is attained during the portal venous phase, which is 60 to 120 seconds after arterial phase enhancement. [10] Tumors supplied from the hepatic artery enhance during the arterial phase, the principle in transarterial chemoembolization and chemoembolization. [11] Arterial embolization and treatment target tumors rather than normal hepatic cells. [12] The hepatic veins enter the inferior vena cava. They can be seen as anechoic tubes with thin walls on ultrasound (see Image. Color Doppler Ultrasound, Hepatic Veins With Hepatic Venous Waveforms). The portal triad, which includes the portal veins, hepatic arteries, and bile ducts surrounded by fibrofatty tissue, can be seen as echogenic foci throughout the liver. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Liver Imaging Reporting and Data System (LI-RADS) is a comprehensive and standardized system for reporting and classifying liver lesions at an increased risk of developing into HCC. [98] LIRAD categorizes lesions into definitely benign, probably benign, probably or malignant, but not HCC specific, intermediate probability of malignancy, probably HCC, and HCC. It guides radiologists, clinicians, and surgeons for surveillance guidelines and recommendations if a biopsy is necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Introduction -- Liver Anatomy and Histology
retrieved_docs.content: The liver's blood supply, primarily from the portal vein, enhances liver imaging during the portal venous phase—a property crucial for diagnostic purposes. Tumors supplied by the hepatic artery exhibit enhanced imaging during arterial phases, a principle utilized in therapeutic interventions like transarterial chemoembolization. Hepatic veins appear as anechoic tubes on ultrasound and drain into the IVC. The portal triad—consisting of portal veins, hepatic arteries, and bile ducts—manifests as echogenic foci within the liver parenchyma. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Intravenous Contrast -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of contrast is the foundation upon which imaging rests. Contrast is simply the ability to distinguish 2 objects. Medical imaging allows adjacent substances or tissue to be distinguishable and visualized. Contrast allowed Marie Curie to identify her bones from the outline of her hand. [1] The ability to distinguish target tissue from the surrounding structures is how anatomy is defined, pathology is identified, and diagnoses are made. [2] While specific anatomical structures have inherent contrast due to their physical properties, others are naturally poorly delineated. See Image. Computed Tomography With Contrast. Radiographs easily define bones against a background of muscle and fat, but identifying early hepatocellular carcinoma from a background of a nodular cirrhotic liver is limited using routine computed tomography imaging. [3] To increase the contrast of these very similar tissues, an agent can change the appearance of the target tissue, the background tissue, or both. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Introduction -- Liver Anatomy and Histology
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic veins run between adjacent segments, ultimately draining into the IVC, while the middle hepatic vein delineates the liver's right and left lobes. The right hepatic vein further partitions the right lobe into posterior and anterior segments, while the falciform ligament separates the left lobe into medial and lateral segments. The portal vein horizontally divides segments into superior and inferior sections. Segment I is the caudate lobe, distinguished from other lobes by its unique characteristics, such as a dual blood supply and direct drainage into the IVC. Segments II, III, and IV constitute the left hepatic lobe sections. Segments V, VI, VII, and VIII constitute the right lobe hepatic sections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: disease include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), partially excised liver anatomy procedures, and ultrasound‑guided examinations, all classified under diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract or of the abdomen/retroperitoneum. The liver also functions as an indirect site for non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures. Overall, the network highlights the interrelationships among abdominal procedures, biliary imaging, liver pathology, detoxification therapies, and their diagnostic modalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and hepatocellular carcinoma. Hepatocellular carcinoma represents a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intra‑hepatic bile ducts, and it is further related to cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), illustrating a spectrum from malignant to non‑malignant hepatic disorders. Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures targeting the liver include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), performed on partially excised hepatic tissue, and ultrasound examination of the liver (فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية), which is a child procedure of diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract. The liver also serves as an indirect site for various non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic interventions. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate the anatomical and functional integration of the gallbladder, liver, and biliary system; the utility of ultrasonographic techniques for biliary and hepatic pathology; the role of substances as causative agents for renal and hepatic toxicities; and the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract is an imaging modality employed to evaluate the abdominal organs and structures. Within this broader category, the liver ultrasound (فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية) is recognized as a specific child examination that focuses on assessing hepatic anatomy, detecting liver lesions, and monitoring hepatic disease. This hierarchical relationship underscores that the liver ultrasound is a specialized component of the overall gastrointestinal tract diagnostic ultrasound, contributing to a comprehensive abdominal assessment in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), and biliary disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic ultrasound of gastrointestinal tract is a diagnostic imaging procedure that is performed directly on the gastrointestinal system, enabling visualization of the organs and structures within the gastrointestinal tract for clinical assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: liver, and biliary system; the utility of ultrasonographic techniques for biliary and hepatic pathology; the role of substances as causative agents for renal and hepatic toxicities; and the liver’s essential functions in detoxification, inflammation, malignancy, and both diagnostic and therapeutic clinical pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2251
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, referred to as játra, can function as an indirect site for a range of other non‑or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, indicating that it may be affected or involved in treatments that are not primarily directed at the gastrointestinal system. In contrast, the liver is also a direct site for the diagnostic procedure فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية (ultrasound examination of the liver), which specifically targets hepatic tissue for imaging and assessment. This dual role underscores the liver’s relevance both as a secondary participant in broader therapeutic interventions and as a primary focus of dedicated hepatic imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 78
retrieved_docs.content: ultrasound offers complementary imaging of gastrointestinal and hepatic structures. Understanding the hierarchical and functional relationships among these procedures supports comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care within gastroenterology and related surgical specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal findings on the chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In turn, alcoholic cirrhosis encompasses hepatic steatosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِري) as a related hepatic manifestation. This relationship underscores that radiographic abnormalities of the thorax can reflect underlying alcohol‑related liver disease, and that the progression of alcoholic liver injury often includes fatty infiltration of the liver (hepatic steatosis) before or alongside the development of cirrhosis. Recognizing these connections aids clinicians in linking chest imaging findings to potential liver pathology and in monitoring the spectrum of alcohol‑induced hepatic damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: cirrhosis (تشَمُّع كَبِدي) and is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2683
retrieved_docs.content: The US scan of gallbladder is a diagnostic procedure that is performed directly on the organ identified as žlučník. Biliary tract disease denotes pathological conditions that are located in the same organ, žlučník. Recognizing that the gallbladder (žlučník) serves both as the direct procedural site for the ultrasound scan and as the anatomical location of biliary tract disease underscores the relevance of targeted imaging in the assessment and management of these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: role of sonography in abdominal disease evaluation. Overall, the network portrays abdominal CT as a well‑defined subclass of general CT imaging, interconnected with contrast‑enhanced variants, related radiologic modalities (plain X‑ray, ultrasound, head CT), and broader diagnostic coding systems, while being organized hierarchically under body‑specific CT categories to guide clinicians in selecting appropriate imaging strategies for diagnosis, staging, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, identified in English as “biopsy of liver,” in Arabic as “خزعة الكبد,” and in Czech as “játra,” is linked to a specific diagnostic intervention. The term “biopsy of liver” classifies the Arabic phrase “خزعة الكبد,” indicating that both refer to the same liver biopsy procedure. The anatomical entity “játra” is described as having a partially excised anatomy procedure called “خزعة الكبد,” meaning that a liver biopsy involves the partial removal of liver tissue. Moreover, “játra” is noted as an indirect procedure site for other non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, highlighting that the liver can serve as a target for a variety of therapeutic interventions beyond those directly involving the gastrointestinal tract. This integrated view clarifies multilingual terminology and underscores the broader procedural relevance of the liver in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: and excision procedures of the central nervous system are related to the skull (lebka), which in turn connects to other non‑open or closed therapeutic nervous‑system procedures. Overall, the liver’s detoxifying function, its disease spectrum, and its imaging modalities are tightly integrated with gastrointestinal and esophageal diagnostic and therapeutic interventions, while the skull serves as an anatomical reference for certain central‑nervous‑system procedures.
